comments.body: You have to have the algorithm or be given the time complexity to know this
It's too bad the source code isn't available for these classes, or you could look it up and find out exactly how they work.
@ColinDeClue but what if it will be an unknown input? profiling will never replace algorithms and time complexity

answers.body:
They do different things.

Collections.reverse takes a mutable list and reverses its order. It takes linear time. It has to.

Guava's Lists.reverse returns a view of the list that is reversed. It returns in constant time, but you'll pay the (small) overhead of the view for each operation.

comments.body: This is not an answer to the question that was asked.

@MarkRotteveel The Question asks about Google Guava com.google.common.collect.Lists.reverse(). This Answer explains that java.util.List#reversed supplants that Guava feature. And the Question asks about speed. This Answer uses the word “fast” three times.

No, you're going off on a tangent about a new feature in Java that does the same or similar thing as Guava's Lists.reverse(), but you're not answering the actual question, which was "Collections.reverse() vs Lists.reverse() which one is faster?" In other words, you seem to use the question as a starting point to blog about a new feature, instead of answering a question.

answers.body:
tl;dr

In Java 21+, get a view onto your list in reverse order by calling java.util.List#reversed (very fast as no re-ordering is actually performed):

myList.reversed()

The above has the same effect as Google Guava com.google.common.collect.Lists.reverse().

For a reversed unmodifiable list that is separate from the original (not a view), the simplest and possibly fastest is a one-liner:

List.copyOf( new ArrayList <>( myList ) ).reversed()

List#reversed

Java 21 brought sequenced collections (video).

ArrayList, List, etc. now inherit from SequencedCollection. That interface requires a method reversed. That method returns a reverse-ordered view of the collection. (Similar to Lists.reverse in Google Guava.)

Pros

👉🏽 This will be much faster than Collections.reverse() since no re-ordering is actually occurring.

And, this approach works on a unmodifiable list since no re-ordering is actually occurring.

Cons

As a mere view, the new reversed list is the original list. So a change to the original also changes the reversed list. This may be a pro or a con depending on your particular situation.

If you want the reversed list to be separate, you must copy the list and then get a reversed view. Of course this takes more time. But that time will be about the same or a little less than actually reversing a list. (Time taken depends on how a list is duplicated within the JVM, with optimization details unknown to me.)

List < String > namesAlphabetical = List.of( "Alice" , "Bob" , "Carol" , "Davis" );
List < String > namesAlphabeticalReversedSeparated =
        new ArrayList <>( namesAlphabetical ).reversed();

Or make the resulting reversed list unmodifiable by calling List.copyOf. Beware: You cannot skip the step of constructing a new list, not if you want a guarantee that he final result is a separate collection. The List.copyOf may be a near no-op if the original collection is already unmodifiable. Javadoc says:

Implementation Note:

If the given Collection is an unmodifiable List, calling copyOf will generally not create a copy.

In that case you would be back to using a mere view rather than a separate collection. So if you want a separate unmodifiable reversed list, (1) construct a new ArrayList with elements from your original, (2) pass to List.copyOf to make it unmodifiable, and (3) get a reversed view. We know the resulting collection will not be changing, despite being a view, because the reference to its original (our newly constructed and modifiable ArrayList) is lost in the æther.

List < String > namesAlphabeticalReversedSeparated =
        List.copyOf( new ArrayList <>( namesAlphabetical ) ).reversed();  // Produces a separate unmodifiable reversed list.

If you are absolutely certain that the original list is modifiable, then you can skip the construction of a new list.

List < String > myReversedSeparateUnmodifiableList =
        List.copyOf( myModifiableList ).reversed();  // Produces a separate unmodifiable reversed list **if** the original is modifiable.

By the way, if appropriate to your situation, you can refer to a List as the more general SequencedCollection in Java 21+.

SequencedCollection < String > names = … // Every `List` is also a `SequencedCollection`.

Example

List < String > namesAlphabetical = List.of( "Alice" , "Bob" , "Carol" , "Davis" );
List < String > namesAlphabeticalReversed = namesAlphabetical.reversed();  // Generate a view onto the original list. No re-ordering actually occurs.

Dump to console.

System.out.println( "namesAlphabetical = " + namesAlphabetical );
System.out.println( "namesAlphabeticalReversed = " + namesAlphabeticalReversed );

When run:

namesAlphabetical = [Alice, Bob, Carol, Davis]
namesAlphabeticalReversed = [Davis, Carol, Bob, Alice]

title: Collections.reverse() vs Lists.reverse() which one is faster?
body: I am interested in following question: Collections.reverse() vs Lists.reverse() which one is faster?
